instructor.name: Dr. Jangho Park
instructor.office_hours: 01:00 PM - 03:00 PM Thursdays, or by appointment

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
This course introduces principles of human factors and ergonomics (HFE), emphasizing human capabilities, limitations, and tendencies, and design to support humans in sociotechnical work systems.
Topics include human information processing, physiological and biomechanical functioning, and implications for design of the workplace and jobs in that workplace. The field of HFE is interdisciplinary, with applications wherever humans interact with equipment in a system context. Examples will be drawn from many domains that involve humans interacting in systems, including manufacturing, medicine, aerospace, ground transportation, and computer systems.

chunks.title: Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Students will be able to ...
- demonstrate an understanding of the unique factors effecting human performance in sociotechnical systems,
- apply human factors theory to the design or selection of system elements to support human performance and safety,
- apply theory to calculate the severity of hazards due to exposure to visual, auditory, vibratory, and other environmental stimuli and identify redesign solutions for mitigating the hazards,
- apply theory to determine ideal display, control, and physical layout characteristics for supporting an operator's cognitive and physical performance, and
- demonstrate applied knowledge of course concepts in identifying human-systems interaction problems with an existing technology, and in proposing redesign solutions.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in MEEN 221 (Statics and Particle Dynamics)

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Designing for People: An Introduction to Human Factors Engineering, Third Edition.
(2017). Lee, J.D., Wickens, C.D., Liu, Y., & Ng Boyle, L.
- Text is strongly recommended. The First and Second editions of “An Introduction to Human Factors Engineering" (Wickens is lead author) are acceptable substitutes
- All other course materials will be hosted on Canvas: https://canvas.tamu.edu/

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grade Determination:
10% Attendance
15% Quizzes
25% Homework
25% Midterm exam
25% Final exam

Grades will be determined on the basis of total points earned. Grades may be curved based on class average such that the point thresholds may be lowered from the following standard:
A: 90-100%
B: 80-89%
C: 70-79%
D: 60-69%
F: 59% and lower

chunks.title: Attendance:
chunks.content: Attendance is required and will be taken each class session. Up to two unexcused absences are allowed without penalty. Three or more unexcused absences will result in a reduction of the attendance grade. Excused absences must be documented and communicated to the instructor as soon as possible (see Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Quizzes:
chunks.content: Approximately 15 short quizzes will be given throughout the semester to assess understanding of course material. Quizzes will include true/false, multiple-choice, short-answer, and calculation questions and will be given during class. The total quiz grade will be based on total points earned, not on the average of percentage grades for each quiz. Make-up quizzes will be provided only in cases of excused absences.

chunks.title: Homework:
chunks.content: There will be approximately 10 homework assignments in total, designed to give you practice in applying principles and ideas learned in the course. Homework assignments will be posted on Canvas and announced in lecture and/or via email. Due dates for each assignment will be given when announced but will generally be due within 1 week of being assigned. Submit completed assignments by the due date via Canvas. The total homework grade will be based on total points, not on the average of percentage grades for each homework.

chunks.title: Exams:
chunks.content: There will be two exams, each worth 25% of the overall course grade. Collaboration on exams is not allowed and will result in offending parties receiving no credit on the exam (see Academic Integrity Statement and Policy Section below for further consequences). The exams will emphasize material discussed in lecture and practiced in the homework; material exclusively in the text will not be tested but may aid in providing background information. Each exam can include true/false, multiple-choice, short-answer, long answer, and calculation questions. Grades will be posted on Canvas and students can review their graded exams during office hours. Exam dates will be flexible only in case of sickness, military service, jury duty, presentation at a professional conference, or death in the family (documentation required in all cases).

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Assignments are due at 11:59 PM on the date specified in Canvas unless a different due date and time are announced. Homework assignments submitted after the due date and time will receive a zero.

chunks.title: Re-grading Policy
chunks.content: Students have 1 week after grades are released for a homework assignment or exam to submit a re-grade request in writing. This request must not exceed 1 page (11-point font, single spacing), and must clearly indicate the relevant problem(s) and justification for why you think re-grading is warranted. Note that the instructors reserve the right to re-grade the entire work which may result in point deduction.

chunks.title: Important Dates
chunks.content:
- January 12: First day of classes
- January 16: Last day to add/drop courses
- January 19: Martin Luther King, Jr. day, no classes
- March 9 - 13: Spring break, no classes
- April 3: Reading day, no classes
- April 14: Last day (by 5 p.m.) to drop courses with no penalty (Q-drop) or to officially withdraw from the University
- April 28: Last day of classes
- April 29: Reading day, no classes
- April 30 - May 5: Final exams
- May 11: Final grades due
Source - https://registrar.tamu.edu/Academic-Calendar

chunks.title: Plagiarism Policy
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using standard citation styles. AI text generators (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to:
- Creating or revising drafts
- Editing your work
- Reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.
Such use of AI text generators in this manner could be considered plagiarism and cheating according to Student Rule 20. More information may also be found at https://aggiehonor.tamu.edu or you may contact your instructor if you have questions.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content: Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.
